coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted one-step synthesis of white light-emitting carbon dot suspensions
coredata.dc:creator: Hinterberger, Vanessa
Wang, Wenshuo
Damm, Cornelia
Wawra, Simon
Thoma, Martin
Peukert, Wolfgang
coredata.dc:description: In this contribution, we demonstrate that an aqueous solution with adjustable fluorescent color, including white light emission, can be achieved by a rapid one-step microwave synthesis method resulting in a mixture of blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) and the yellow-emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Aqueous mixtures of o-phenylene-diamine (oPD) and citric acid (CA) are used as precursors. The resulting product structures are analyzed by FT-IR and NMR spectroscopy and the size of the resulting CDs is determined by atomic force microscopy to be 1.1 ± 0.3 nm. The synthesized solution exhibits two fluorescence emission peaks at 430 and 560 nm, which were found to originate from the CDs and DAP, respectively. The intensity ratio of both fluorescence peaks depends on pH, which is driven by the protonation state of DAP. In consequence, the fluorescence emission color of the CD solution can be tuned precisely and reproducibly from blue to white to yellow by careful control of the pH. Finally, at a pH level of 5.4, at which there is equal blue and yellow emission intensity, a white light emitting solution can be successfully produced in a very fast and simple synthesis procedure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave-assisted hydrothermal method
N-doped carbon dots
White-light emission
pH-dependent fluorescence color
Diaminophenazine

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the synthesized CD/DAP structures
Optical properties of the synthesized CD/DAP solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported financially by the German Science Foundation (DFG) , Collaborative Research Center SFB953 : “Synthetic Carbon Allotropes”. The author would also like to thank the group of Prof. Guldi of the Institute Physical Chemistry I for the helpful discussion and Dr. Schulz of the Institute Chemical Engineering I for the possibility to do NMR measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The formation of the CDs: Citric acid (CA) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD) are forming the 2-pyridone structure while CA creates the carbon core in a pyrolytic reaction [24]. (b) Oxidative dimerization of oPD forming the stable 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) [44–49].
FT-IR spectra (a) DAP synthesized by microwave irradiation (b) CD/DAP solution synthesized by microwave irradiation at pH 5.4.
(a) AFM height profile; (b) Typical AFM image of the CDs.
UV-Vis spectrum of DAP for different pH values.
Three different protonation degrees of DAP with all resonance structures.
UV-Vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CD/DAP solution.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the synthesized white emitting CD/DAP solution: at 400 nm excitation wavelength, (b) 3D-Plot from 250 to 450 nm excitation wavelengths, (c) Matlab calculations using the CIE co-ordinate calculator indicate white light emission for 400 nm excitation wavelength.

3D PL spectra of CD/DAP solutions show increasing yellow fluorescence peak and shrinking blue fluorescence peak with increasing pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence images (excitation wavelength 400 nm) show varying emission wavelengths at different pH values (b) Fluorescence emission color scheme at different pH values calculated by Matlab CIE co-ordinate calculator. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Maximum fluorescence intensity of CD and DAP as a function of pH (b) Intensity relation of blue emission (430 nm) to yellow emission (560 nm) as a function of pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Testing the reversibility of emission color control of synthesized CD solutions by tuning the pH. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Photostability of DAP during exposure of UV light (365 nm).

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted one-step synthesis of white light-emitting carbon dot suspensions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Erlangen-Nürnberg, Institute of Particle Technology, Cauerstraße 4, D-91058, Erlangen, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Center of Functional Particle Systems, Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg, Habertstraße 9a, 91058, Erlangen, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Center of Functional Particle Systems

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Friedrich-Alexander-Universität Erlangen-Nürnberg, Institute of Particle Technology, Cauerstraße 4, D-91058, Erlangen, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this contribution, we demonstrate that an aqueous solution with adjustable fluorescent color, including white light emission, can be achieved by a rapid one-step microwave synthesis method resulting in a mixture of blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) and the yellow-emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). Aqueous mixtures of o-phenylene-diamine (oPD) and citric acid (CA) are used as precursors. The resulting product structures are analyzed by FT-IR and NMR spectroscopy and the size of the resulting CDs is determined by atomic force microscopy to be 1.1 ± 0.3 nm. The synthesized solution exhibits two fluorescence emission peaks at 430 and 560 nm, which were found to originate from the CDs and DAP, respectively. The intensity ratio of both fluorescence peaks depends on pH, which is driven by the protonation state of DAP. In consequence, the fluorescence emission color of the CD solution can be tuned precisely and reproducibly from blue to white to yellow by careful control of the pH. Finally, at a pH level of 5.4, at which there is equal blue and yellow emission intensity, a white light emitting solution can be successfully produced in a very fast and simple synthesis procedure.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have received significant scientific attention due to their high fluorescence, unique optical properties, low toxicity and high biocompatibility [1–5]. CDs prepared by environmentally-friendly processes show similar fluorescence properties and present a promising economical alternative to conventional toxic metal-based quantum dots (QDs) [6]. In consequence, the investigation of CDs were driven forward and different application possibilities were tested [7,8]. The vast majority of CDs emit light in the blue range of the visible spectrum [8]. Therefore, the question arises whether it is possible to create different fluorescent colors by combining CDs with organic fluorophores and, in particular, to create white light for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). Semiconductor QDs are currently mainly used for the production of WLEDs. At the moment, there are only two widely-used methods for creating white color emission: the additive RGB method which combines different color-emitting QDs (red, green, blue) to achieve white light and the phosphor method [9]. The RGB method is limited by the fact that it requires challenging processes with toxic substances and shows a poor color rendering index [10]. The phosphor method is based on the concept that the combination of blue and yellow appear white to the eye and involves combining blue LED light with a yellow phosphor layer [11]. Nevertheless, toxic materials are still needed in this case and waste disposal of heavy metals used in the LEDs must also be considered [12]. The use of CDs as a fluorescent material for WLEDs is a promising solution to this problem [13] and the idea to obtain white light from the combination of a blue and yellow light emitter was adopted. Until now, CDs were mostly used as the blue emitter in combination with rare earth luminescent materials [14,15]. Y. Wang et al. showed that it is possible to achieve white light emitting diodes from CDs produced from microwave synthesis. However white light was only achieved after several processing steps, including mixing the freeze dried CDs with silicone and thermal curing of the mixture [16]. In this work, we describe a white light emitting CD solution synthesized within 10–15 min in a one-step microwave synthesis reaction. The solution consists of nitrogen-doped blue fluorescent CDs and 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), a yellow fluorescent dye which were both synthesized simultaneously from mixtures of o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and citric acid (CA). During synthesis, the oPD has two important functions: it behaves as a doping agent to improve the blue fluorescence intensity of the CDs while simultaneously reacting with itself to form the yellow emitting DAP. The microwave-assisted approach is a low cost, energy efficient and facile technique with a very short reaction time of only 5–20 min [17–19]. The blue emitting CDs and yellow emitting DAP expose light of the complementary colors and the combination of both emit pure white light. The suspensions exhibiting two emission peaks in the blue and yellow spectral region and the intensity ratio of both peaks and therefore the emission color is a unique function of pH. White light emission was obtained at a preset pH of 5.4. However after synthesis, the color can be switched reversibly to blue or yellow, by setting the pH beneath or above 5.4. Finally, a white light emitting CD solution could be synthesized very quickly and easily, thus demonstrating the mixing abilities of CDs and the simplicity of achieving a white light emitting aqueous solution by microwave synthesis.

0.144 g of oPD and 0.129 g of CA (both obtained from Carl Roth, Germany) were dissolved in 100 ml ultrapure water, resulting in an oPD:CA molar ratio of 2:1. For getting the white light-emitting combined CD solution, the pH of the mother solution was adjusted from 4.3 to 5.4 by using 0.1 M NaOH. 4 ml of the solution was heated to a temperature of 125 °C for 5 min by a microwave reactor from CEM Discover with a power of 300 W. The reactor was equipped with a reaction tube in an autoclave with a maximum capacity of 80 ml. The product was cooled to room temperature and the remaining solution appeared yellow-orange. The product can be purified with a dialysis membrane with a molecular cut-off of 1000 g/mol (supplied by Spectrum Laboratories Inc., USA). Since DAP is able to pass the dialysis bag, the equilibrium between blue and yellow fluorescence is shifted to blue fluorescence. Therefore the dialysis time should be very short (<30 min) and the pH of the purified solution must be raised after purification to regain white light. As reference pure DAP solution was synthesized by microwave-treading of a solution of 0.216 g of oPD in 100 ml ultrapure water under the same conditions as for the CD synthesis and the obtained product appeared brightly yellow. The pH of the solution was measured in the range of 6–7. For measuring fluorescence quantum yield (QY), size and photostability of the single CDs, the CD/DAP solution was dialyzed for several hours to remove free DAP.

For recording UV-Vis absorption spectra, a Cary 100 (Varian) spectrometer with a range from 200 to 800 nm was used. Fluorescence spectra were measured in a wavelength range from 300 to 800 nm using a Jobin-Yvon photoluminescence spectrometer (Horiba, Japan). All absorbance and fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature using quartz cuvettes with a path length of 10 mm. Fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were determined using the slope method described by Jones et al., Do et al. and Zheng et al. using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.5 M H2SO4 as a standard (Sigma-Aldrich, America, QY: 54.6%) [20–22]. The QY was calculated using the following equation (1):
(1)
ϕ X
=
ϕ s
(
G x
G s
)
(
n x
n s
)
2
where φ is the QY, G is the slope determined by the fitted line in a plot of the integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance at the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index, and subscripts x and s refer to the sample and the standard, respectively. For the white emitting solution at pH 5.4 the QY was calculated to 5.4%.

The Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a “Digilab FTS3100” FT-IR spectrometer. The samples were prepared by being pressed to discs with KBr. The height of the CDs was determined using a MultiMode SPM “NanoScope 3” (Digital Instruements/Veeco, Germany) atomic force microscope (AFM). For the measurement, NSC15/AlBS cantilevers (frequency 325 kHz, spring constant 46 Nm−1 and nominal tip radius < 10 nm) were used in the tapping mode. In order to prepare CDs samples for AFM size measurements, the CD/DAP solution was dialyzed for several hours and the CD solution was dropped on a clean Mica sheet (Plano, Germany) and dried at room temperature. The 1H NMR spectra were measured using a JEOL ECX 400 MHz spectrometer with a DMSO‑d 6-inset as internal standard. The deuterated solvent was CDCl3. For size characterization of the particles in solution, sedimentation velocity experiments at 55 000 rpm for 36 h at 25 °C were conducted in an analytical ultracentrifuge (AUC) “Optima L-90 K” (Beckman Coulter, USA) equipped with a multi wavelength (MWL)-detector. The related background information can be found in the literature [23]. The photostability of the CDs and DAP solutions was measured by using a 365 nm UV-lamp from Consort VL-6.L with the power of 24 W.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the synthesized CD/DAP structures
ce:section.ce:para: It has been previously shown, that CA is able to react with α,β-diamines to form fluorescent CDs of high QY [24,24–26]. There is much debate in the literature about the structure of CDs and their origin of fluorescence emission. The carboxylic acid groups of CA undergo condensation reactions with the amino groups of the α,β-diamines, resulting in the formation of polyamides. Crosslinking, dehydration and irreversible carbonization of these polymer structures at temperatures >200 °C leads to formation of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon cores containing pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen [24,25,27–33]. It was shown that the QY of CDs increases with the amount of pyridinc nitrogen [31]. It is also proposed that CA is capable of ring closure upon reaction with several α,β-diamines such as oPD resulting in a corresponding pyridone derivate [24,25]. Accordingly, the formation of a fluorescent 2-pyridone species, a low-molecular fluorophore (Fig. 1 (a)) and the associated reaction mechanism was proposed (Fig. S1(a)) [24,26,34,35]. Moreover CA undergoes a pyrolytic polycondensation reaction leading to formation of carbon cores consisting of small graphene oxide like units (Fig. 1(a)) [28]. The low-molecular fluorophores can then be attached or integrated into the carbon core [26,30,35]. The group of Fang et al. proposed that the pyridone-molecules are connected covalently to the CD surface due to condensation reactions, providing the CDs new energy levels and producing an intrinsic PL signal [25]. In summary, blue-emitting CDs consist of an aromatic carbon skeleton formed by CA condensation, fluorophores located on the CD surface and/or fluorescent centers in the core (Fig. 1(a)) [24,26,36–38]. The dominant fluorophore structure is not only depending on the starting materials but also on reaction conditions: at low reaction temperatures and/or short reaction times low-molecular pyridone-like fluorophores seem to be the dominant fluorescence mechanism of CDs [30]. In this case the CDs exhibit high QY, the fluorescence spectrum is excitation-wavelength independent and the CDs are susceptible to photobleaching [24,30,34,35]. In contrast the fluorescence of CDs synthesized under harsher conditions is ascribed more to the carbon core. In that case excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra are observed for wavelengths >380 nm and the QY is mostly <20% [30,38]. Reasons for the excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence spectra, quantum confinement as well as trap or molecular states on the surface are proposed and discussed [30]. If oPD is used as starting material besides CA the reaction mechanism is even more complex than discussed above as solutions of the free base oPD are susceptible to dimerization (or even polymerization) under oxidizing conditions (such as in the presence of air) [39]. This reaction is accelerated by light due to the photo-induced formation of oxygen radicals. The mechanism of oPD oxidation is shown in Fig. 1(b). In aqueous solutions the main product is 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). However in low amounts, higher oligomers of oPD might be also formed subsequently (Fig. S1(b)) [39,40]. As a result, oPD takes part in two reactions which occur simultaneously in solution: the reaction with CA to form the blue-emitting 2-pyridone structure and an oxidative dimerization with itself to form DAP, which displays yellow fluorescence emission (Fig. 1(b)) [30,39].

The simultaneous production of blue-emitting CDs and yellow emitting DAP by a one-step microwave synthesis reaction results in a homogenous solution which shows white light fluorescence emission at a pH of 5.4. In order to study the proposed mixture of CDs and DAP, spectroscopic measurement methods including FT-IR and NMR spectroscopy were carried out. FT-IR spectroscopy was able to detect the expected functional groups of the products: Fig. 2 (a) shows the FT-IR spectrum of pure DAP, which was synthesized by microwave irradiation of oPD. Fig. 2(b) represents the FT-IR spectrum of the reaction product from CA and oPD which is expected to consist of CDs and DAP. By comparing both spectra, peaks generated by the CDs can be distinguished from the peaks assigned to DAP. As a result, all the important peaks of the DAP and residues of oPD can be seen in both spectra [39,41]. In Fig. 2(a) the two peaks between 3386 and 3359 cm−1 reflect N-H stretching vibrations, which are mainly from residues of oPD whereas the two NH2 signals between 3290 and 3190 cm−1 are generated by DAP [39,41]. The peaks at 1634 and 1590 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of C=N bonds in DAP. All other peaks, such as 1503 cm−1 for aromatic C=C bonds, 1274 cm−1 for aromatic C-N bonds, 1246 cm−1 for aliphatic C-N bonds and 750 cm−1 for out-of-plane bending vibration of 1,2-disubstituted benzene rings, are present in the spectra of DAP and the unreacted precursor oPD [42]. In Fig. 2(b) the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of O-H free hydroxyl groups and overlaps with the peak of NH2 of DAP. The deformation vibration of O-H appears at 1400 cm−1. In the range between 1730 cm−1 and 1503 cm−1 many peaks of the CDs and DAP overlap with each other. However a peak at 1700 cm−1 can be seen and assigned to C=O stretching vibration of carboxylic groups of the CDs. All further peaks at 1586 cm−1, 1504 cm−1, 1241 cm−1 and 748 cm−1 are assigned to DAP (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). The strong decrease of the stretching vibration of 1,2-disubsituted benzene at 748 cm−1 from microwave assisted synthesized DAP (Fig. 2(b)) is attributed to a much lower concentration of unreacted oPD in the reaction solution, as it is consumed for the formation of the CDs. NMR spectroscopy was carried out in order to see the typical signals of DAP in the mixed solution. Fig. S2 (a) in the SI shows the 1H NMR spectrum of the CD/DAP solution. As the CDs were synthesized in water, a very strong signal of H2O at 4.57 ppm can be found. Most signals of the functional groups of the CDs (OH and C=O) and the sp2 carbon network are located in the same range [42]. The 1H NMR spectrum of pure DAP is shown in Fig. S2 (b) and can be compared to the mixture. All typical signals of DAP can be found in the 1H NMR spectrum of CD/DAP. However, due to the high dilution of the synthesized solution, these signals are very weak. Two typical peaks of oPD (6.52 ppm and 6.63 ppm) can also be identified in the spectrum, which confirms that parts of oPD remain unreacted after synthesis [43].

AFM measurements were carried out to obtain the topographic height of the CDs (Fig. 3 ). Fig. S3 shows the size distribution of the measured CDs. The observed height of the particles was in the range of 0.7–2.0 nm with an average height of 1.1 ± 0.3 nm. The CDs within Analytical Ultracentrifugation (AUC) show sedimentation equilibrium without meniscus depletion even at high rotor speeds (Fig. S4). This might be either due to a density close to the solvent or to high diffusion. The latter is in agreement with the size measurement from AFM and can serve as an indicator for sample homogeneity. Fig. S4 in the SI shows the radial extinction profile at the beginning and at the end of the sedimentation velocity experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the synthesized CD/DAP solution
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the UV-Vis spectrum of the microwave synthesized solution of blue-emitting CDs and DAP as a function of pH value. The spectrum presents two signals between pH 3 and 4.5. In comparison with the UV-Vis spectrum of pure DAP in Fig. 4, the absorption bands at 402 nm and 394 nm must have been generated by the CDs in solution. By rising pH value, the signals of the CDs are no longer apparent in the visible spectrum and therefore only the peak of DAP can be followed. Each time that pH is changed, DAP changes to the respective protonated or deprotonated form, comparable to a traditional indicator. During all pH steps, the color of the solution is changing from yellow in the neutral region over yellow-orange to orange in the acidic region. Regarding the color of the solution or rather the absorption signals, it is not important if the pH was adjusted before or after the synthesis (Fig. S5). Fig. S6 shows the spectrum at pH 3, which displays even a third absorption signal at the wavelength of 611 nm. This indicates either that in a very low amount further polymerization is possible and leads to higher oligomers of oPD or it confirms that amino-functionalized CDs expose light also in the visible range [37]. The synthesis of higher amounts of oPD polymerized chains can, however, be neglected, as the viscosity measurement of the solution showed a dynamic viscosity of η = 1.05 mPas which corresponds nearly to that of water, namely η = 1.00 mPas [52]. In order to exclude absorption bands from CA and oPD in the spectrum of the CD/DAP solution (Fig. 6), both UV-Vis spectra were measured at different pH values (Fig. S7). CA shows an absorption band at 215 nm. Its position is not affected by changes in the pH (Fig. S7(a)). The absorption bands of oPD show pH dependence similar to DAP (protonation or deprotonation of NH2), however the affected absorption bands do not appear in the visible range and do not influence the UV-Vis Spectrum of CD/DAP (Fig. S7(b)).

Fig. 7 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the white light-emitting solution of CDs and DAP. Fig. 7(a) displays the PL spectrum at 400 nm excitation wavelength. In Fig. 7(b) the 3D PL mapping spectrum for 250–450 nm excitation wavelengths is shown. The spectra exhibit two emission peaks at 430 and 560 nm, which belong to the blue-emitting CDs and the yellow-emitting DAP, respectively [50]. Both emission peaks are independent on excitation wavelength (Fig. 7). By using a molar ratio (oPD:CA) of 2:1 and an adjusted pH of 5.4, the PL spectrum with 400 nm excitation wavelength shows equal intensities for both emission peaks (Fig. 7(a)). As shown in Fig. 7(c), the Commission Internationale d’Eclairage (CIE) co-calculator gives white light with chromaticity coordinates of (0.32, 0.33). Pure white light has the coordinates (0.33, 0.33), proving that pure white light has been generated by a one-pot synthesis of CDs and DAP [15]. In order to check the reproducibility of the microwave synthesis 20-30 batches of the CD/DAP solutions were synthesized under similar conditions. In all cases it was possible to achieve white light emission. However, as the microwave reactor shows fluctuations in reaction time and temperature, for some batches the pH had to be readjusted after synthesis to fall in the range from 5.2 to 5.5.

Fig. S8 shows that with a longer residence time during microwave reaction, the synthesis of the blue-emitting CDs is favored. The opposite effect is noticed for shorter residence times, giving the spectrum of the pure yellow emission of DAP. Moreover, the synthesis of CDs is also preferred by using a higher reaction temperature. This leads to the conclusion that the CDs need more energy and time to be formed, however, DAP can be synthesized at moderate conditions [50]. Table S1 shows an overview of all parameters affecting the equilibrium between CD and DAP. In order to achieve white light, it is necessary to separately adjust these parameters for each microwave reactor.

In the following, we demonstrate how to tailor the emission by tuning the pH value of the dispersion after the microwave synthesis. Fig. 8 shows the PL spectra of the mixed CD/DAP solution for the pH values between 3 and 9. For pH values lower than 5.4, the intensity of the blue emission peak at 430 nm increases whereas the intensity of the yellow emission peak at 560 nm decreases. The opposite effect is noticed for all pH values above 5.4. This means, for pH values lower or higher than 5.4 the emission color is blue or yellow, respectively. By adjusting the pH to lower (for blue emission) or higher (for yellow emission) values, the color intensity will increase. The trend in the emission color is shown in Fig. 9 (a). Since the color of the synthesized solution is yellow, the white light of the fluorescent solution can not be seen with the naked eye at pH 5.4. However, at lower pH values between 4.9 and 5.0 the white light of the fluorescent solution is visible. The pH range for a visible color change is approximately located between pH 4 und 6. The sensitivity in this range is very high (Fig. 9(a) and (b)).

In order to see how the pH value effects the emission intensity of CD and DAP simultaneously, the maximum intensity for the CDs at 400 nm/430 nm and for DAP at 400 nm/560 nm (excitation/emission wavelength) and the resulting intensity ratio for blue to yellow emission was plotted against the pH value (Fig. 10 (a) and (b)). The solution was synthesized at pH 5.4 and the pH was changed after synthesis firstly to lower and then to higher values. In Fig. 10 (a) it can be seen, that in the range of pH 4–8, the fluorescence intensity of DAP is increasing to a maximum at pH 7. In order to explain the low fluorescence intensity of DAP in acidic media, the structure change of DAP shown in Fig. 5 has to be taken into account. It could be deduced that, the positive charge and the lower electron density of DAP must lower the fluorescence emission intensity and raises the oxidation potential [53]. The PL intensity change is completely reversible due to reversible protonation and deprotonation of the DAP structure. Therefore the fluorescence color of the solution is mainly controlled by the amount of protonated or deprotonated DAP. The CDs do not influence the pH-dependency of the PL intensity of DAP, as the same behavior of DAP could be seen without CDs. At pH 5.4, corresponding to the original solution after the synthesis, the CDs showed the highest PL intensity. Upon increase or decrease in pH, the intensity of the CDs declined in both cases reaching its minimum at pH 8.1. The protonation or deprotonation affects the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. After each pH change, certain amounts of CDs were affected and damaged. Therefore, the pH-sensitivity of the CDs limits the number of fluorescence color changes of the solution. Furthermore the presence of DAP influences the fluorescence intensity of the CDs as DAP shows quenching abilities: Fig. S9 (b) shows a decreasing fluorescence intensity of blue emitting CDs after one day of storage which were diluted with a DAP containing aqueous solution. As a reference, the sample of Fig. S9 (a) shows the same CD dilution in water without DAP which was completely stable after the same time of storage.

In order to explain the quenching effect of DAP, two different processes have to be considered. First of all, it is possible that the CDs and DAP interact with each other in a photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process. Both compounds possess a considerable number of π-bonds which lead to π-π stacking and adsorption of DAP on the CD surface [54,55]. In this electron transfer process the electron which is excited in the CDs is donated to DAP. The group of Y. Zhang at al. already showed that nanospheres and nanorods of poly(m-phenylenediamine) were able to quench a fluorescent dye attached to ssDNA [54]. The same process could be applied to CDs which are excellent electron donors [56,57]. The second possibility is a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism that describes a non-radiative energy transfer between two fluorophores through dipole-dipole coupling [58]. This process is mainly probable if the blue fluorescence originates from small effective free fluorophores. Certainly, the quenching mechanism explains the low QY of the CDs in the white emitting solution with a total QY of 5.4%. Therefore, it would be necessary to protect the blue emitting CDs from quenching effects of DAP. If DAP was filtered out, the QY of the blue CDs increases after 2 h of dialysis to 29.7%. Hence, in a system where both components exist next to each other, a suitable method of CD surface protection is necessary. The results could be reproduced by simply mixing blue emitting CDs with DAP by hand (Fig. S10). The mixed solution shows both emission peaks and therefore DAP can be mixed with a variety of CDs (Fig. S10(c)).

Upon certain changes in pH, the fluorescence color of the one-pot synthesized solution changes reversibly to blue, then white and finally yellow consecutively. Therefore, it is possible to create a desired fluorescence color by only adjusting the pH. The reversible color change of an acidic, blue-emitting and a neutral, yellow-emitting solution to white light emission is shown in Fig. 11 and demonstrates how the desired fluorescence color can be set by pH.

The photostability of CDs and DAP was measured by irradiation with UV-light (365 nm) in a time range of 24 h. Fig. 12 shows the PL intensity of DAP during illumination. It can be seen that the PL intensity is decreasing until reaching a plateau. A similar trend was found for CDs where the fluorescence originates from small fluorophores [26,37]. The result shows that the effect of the photobleaching mechanism can be transferred to the fluorophore DAP, as oxygen radicals may attack DAP in a similar way.

The CD solution was separated from DAP by dialysis in order to measure the photobleaching activity of both components separately. Nearly no yellow fluorescence could be seen after 2–3 h of purification, meaning that most of the DAP was filtered out of the membrane at this point. After 2 h illumination with UV light, only the fluorescence of the blue fluorophores was destroyed, whereas the yellow fluorescence was nearly unaffected (Fig. S11 a-b). When the solution was dialyzed for 18–36 h the result was even more surprising: the blue fluorescence intensity peak decreased remarkably. Moreover, it was not possible to remove all of the DAP from the solution. This result shows that small blue fluorophores are also able to pass the dialysis bag easily. It would thus be conceivable that not only blue emitting fluorophores but also some of the DAP is bound to the CDs. Fig. S12 shows how the PL spectrum changes after 18 h of dialysis. The low-molecular blue fluorophores pass through the membrane, leaving the bigger carbon core behind which shows an excitation dependent spectrum (Fig. S12(b)). Moreover, upon exposure of UV-light (Fig. S11), DAP or pre-stages of DAP which are probably connected to the CDs, are released during photobleaching. It is also imaginable that loosely bounded blue fluorophores are destroyed and are thus able to react further on to DAP in a process which is also initiated by light [40].

In summary, we propose that the blue fluorescence of the CDs, synthesized in this study, originates mainly from low-molecular 2-pyridone-type fluorophores which are connected to the surface of CDs and also freely floating in solution. The excitation-wavelength independent fluorescence spectra confirms the assumption of intrinsic emission from the effective fluorophores which can be easily quenched due to the FRET effect which is most likely the dominant quenching mechanism [24–26]. The quite mild synthesis conditions and the small CD size as determined from the AFM measurements, also confirm the assumption that mainly low-molecular fluorophores are synthesized (Fig. 1(a)) [30,34,37]. The dialysis experiments show that both DAP and the blue emitting fluorophores are able to pass the dialysis membrane. The yellow emitting DAP shows a typical photobleaching behavior which was also found for blue fluorophores of CDs [37]. DAP cannot be removed completely from solution by dialysis, leading to the conclusion that parts of it are bound to the CDs.

Herein, we demonstrate the possibility of synthesizing solutions with white fluorescence, and even adjustable color emission, via a fast and simple one-step microwave process, using aqueous mixtures of o-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors. The synthesized products are investigated by FT-IR and NMR and are identified to consist of a mixture of blue-emitting CDs and the yellow-emitting organic DAP. The average size of the CDs was determined by AFM to be 1.1 ± 0.3 nm, which is in agreement with the observed sedimentation behavior in AUC analysis. The PL spectra of the synthesized product solutions contain a blue and a yellow emission center, which was found to originate from the CDs and DAP, respectively. UV-Vis measurements demonstrate that DAP behaves as an organic pH indicator, displaying three different absorption bands at different protonation states, including three different colors of the solution. The intensity of the yellow emission center is shown to be heavily dependent on the protonation state of DAP and can thus be easily tuned by varying the pH. This tuneability of the DAP fluorescence center enables reversible fluorescence color alterations of the synthesized product solutions between blue and white and yellow for a certain number of pH changes. Ultimately, through careful control of the solution pH, we show that it is possible to produce a white-emitting CD solution. In order to further improve the white light emission and increase the QY for a variety of potential LED applications, it is necessary to protect the blue emitting CDs from the quenching effect of DAP. Furthermore in order to check the mixing abilities of CDs more intensively, organic yellow fluorescent components with higher fluorescence intensities than DAP should be also tested to see if it is possible to achieve diverse white emitting solutions with higher QY. In this work it was already possible to show the simplicity of synthesizing a white-light emitting solution based on CDs and DAP and establish the concept of combining CDs with organic luminescent components for a required luminescent color. Finally, further tests are necessary to achieve materials with higher QY, the final goal being transferring the solution to the solid state and testing white LEDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the German Science Foundation (DFG), Collaborative Research Center SFB953: “Synthetic Carbon Allotropes”. The author would also like to thank the group of Prof. Guldi of the Institute Physical Chemistry I for the helpful discussion and Dr. Schulz of the Institute Chemical Engineering I for the possibility to do NMR measurements.
